summary: Big moves along the BeltLine today with new construction breaking ground in Reynoldstown and Asian-American comfort food arriving in Inman Park. Meanwhile, ICE agents showed up at Hartsfield-Jackson to help with massive TSA lines, though travelers say they're mostly just standing around.

topStory.headline: After 5 Years, Reynoldstown BeltLine Development Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: Construction is finally underway at a long-stalled development near the BeltLine in Reynoldstown. The 85-unit townhome and condo project at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace was cleared five years ago but has been sitting vacant ever since. Atlanta-based Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers and is moving forward with the project about a block from the Eastside Trail.
topStory.body:
You know that empty lot at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace that's been sitting there like a missing tooth for the past five years? Well, it's finally getting some love. Embry Development has taken over what was supposed to be a Toll Brothers project and is moving forward with 85 residential units just a block west of the BeltLine's Eastside Trail.

The site was cleared way back in 2021, and honestly, locals were starting to wonder if anything would ever happen there. It's prime real estate in Reynoldstown, practically next door to all the BeltLine action, but it's been nothing but dirt and weeds for what feels like forever. Embry Development bought the site from Toll Brothers two years ago and kept the original permits, which was smart given how long city approvals can take these days.

What's coming is a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos, which is actually pretty great for the area. We need more homeownership options near the BeltLine that aren't just luxury rentals. The project should help fill in some of the gaps in Reynoldstown while keeping the neighborhood's character intact. Plus, being a block from Memorial Drive puts it right in the sweet spot for walkability.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment and cleared land at the Reynoldstown development site near the BeltLine

stories.headline: Missing Middle Housing Village Opens Near Decatur
stories.summary: A new village-style development of 18 standalone houses is about to debut near Decatur, offering more affordable single-family options in an area where median home values have climbed over $700,000. The Cottages at Midway features houses built around shared courtyards and greenspace, replacing underused lots on Midway Road.
stories.body:
Finally, someone's doing something smart about the housing crunch near Decatur. The Cottages at Midway is a village-style development that's bringing 18 standalone houses to a 2.1-acre site on Midway Road. These aren't your typical suburban cookie-cutter homes, they're designed around shared courtyards and greenspace, which feels way more community-oriented.

With median home values in Decatur pushing past $700,000, this mixed-income approach is exactly what we need. The developers are calling it 'missing middle' housing, and they're right. There's this huge gap between apartments and million-dollar houses, and projects like this help fill that void. Plus, being close to downtown Decatur means you can actually walk to restaurants and shops instead of driving everywhere.

The timing couldn't be better. ITP housing is getting more expensive by the month, and too many new developments are either luxury rentals or McMansions. This feels like a more thoughtful approach that might actually let regular people buy homes near the city without selling a kidney.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan showing the layout of Cottages at Midway development

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Finally Taking Shape
stories.summary: The GW Parkside development is bringing 23 new townhomes to West End along the BeltLine. Developer Ackerman & Co. started construction a year ago, and the first seven units should hit the market this spring. It's rare to find for-sale townhomes in this popular stretch of Southwest Atlanta.
stories.body:
Over in West End, those townhomes near the BeltLine are finally starting to look like actual buildings instead of construction chaos. Ackerman & Co., the same folks behind Lee + White, started this project a year ago at 600 Hopkins Street, and now you can actually see the bones of what's coming.

This is the GW Parkside development (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes), and it's bringing 23 new units to the area. What makes this interesting is that these are for-sale townhomes, which is pretty unusual along the BeltLine these days. Most new construction is either luxury rentals or massive apartment complexes, so having actual homeownership options is refreshing.

The first seven properties should be ready this spring, which is perfect timing for anyone who's been waiting to buy near the BeltLine without paying Ponce City Market prices. West End has been heating up for years, and being able to walk to the Westside Trail from your front door is going to be a major selling point. Plus, Ackerman knows what they're doing with Lee + White, so this should be quality construction.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes in West End

stories.headline: Asian-American Comfort Food Lands in Inman Park Today
stories.summary: Mushi Ni, a new restaurant from chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez, opened today on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park. The pop-up turned permanent spot serves Asian-American comfort food including soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken, and Thai tea martinis, just off the Freedom Park Trail.
stories.body:
There's a new spot in Inman Park that deserves your attention. Mushi Ni opened today on Elizabeth Street, and it's the first restaurant from chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez, who've been testing their Asian-American comfort food concept as a pop-up. Now they've got a permanent home just off the Freedom Park Trail.

The menu hits all the right notes: soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, crab Rangoon puffs, and their signature Peking chicken platters that feed two people for $25. They're also doing Thai tea martinis, which honestly sounds perfect for this neighborhood. Le and Jimenez both have serious restaurant experience (she worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta), so they know what they're doing.

What I love about this is the location. Elizabeth Street has been quietly becoming a little food destination, and being right near the Freedom Park Trail means you can walk or bike there from half of intown. Plus, Inman Park has been needing more casual, creative dining options that aren't just another pizza place or overpriced gastropub. This feels like it fits the neighborhood perfectly.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: NYC Food Hall Mastermind Brings East Side Concept to Life
stories.summary: Robert Montwaid, who created New York's famous Gansevoort Market, is opening his third Atlanta food hall next month. Vela Park Food Works near East Lake Golf Club will feature six chef-driven concepts with craft cocktails and outdoor dining, opening in mid-April.
stories.body:
The guy who basically invented the modern food hall in NYC is about to debut his latest Atlanta project, and it sounds pretty spectacular. Robert Montwaid, the mastermind behind Gansevoort Market, is opening Vela Park Food Works near East Lake Golf Club in mid-April as part of the new Vela Park rental community.

This is Montwaid's third food hall in Atlanta, so he clearly sees something in our market that works. Vela Park Food Works will have six different chef-driven concepts, plus craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor dining space. Given that the East Lake area has been exploding with new development, the timing seems perfect for a high-quality food destination.

What's interesting is how food halls have evolved since Montwaid started this trend. It's not just about throwing a bunch of vendors together anymore, it's about creating an actual dining experience. And with East Lake's proximity to Decatur, East Atlanta Village, and Kirkwood, this could become a real destination rather than just a neighborhood amenity.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works food hall

stories.headline: ICE Agents Hit Atlanta Airport as TSA Lines Stretch for Hours
stories.summary: Immigration agents are now patrolling Hartsfield-Jackson to help with massive security lines caused by the government shutdown. However, travelers report the agents mostly seem to be standing around since they can't actually help with TSA screening. Wait times are still hitting 4+ hours during peak periods.
stories.body:
The situation at Hartsfield-Jackson is getting pretty wild, and not in a good way. ICE agents showed up this week to supposedly help with the crushing TSA lines, but travelers are saying they're basically just standing around looking official. Makes sense, since they can't actually touch TSA equipment or help with security screening.

The real problem is that over 40% of TSA agents have been calling out of work because they're not getting paid during the shutdown. That's left security lines that stretch for literal hours during peak times. Some people are reporting 4-hour waits just to get through the checkpoint, which is absolutely bananas for what's supposed to be the world's most efficient airport.

Mayor Dickens assured everyone that the ICE agents won't be doing immigration enforcement, they're just there for 'general security support.' But honestly, what everyone really needs is for Congress to get its act together and end this shutdown so TSA workers can get paid and come back to work. Having armed federal agents wandering around an already stressed airport isn't exactly making travelers feel more comfortable.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents patrolling Atlanta airport terminal during government shutdown

stories.headline: Decatur's Bike Lane Project Finally Nearing Completion
stories.summary: After nearly a decade of planning, Decatur's Complete Streets project on West Howard Avenue is almost finished. The one-mile stretch now features protected bike lanes, new sidewalks, and traffic calming measures from downtown Decatur to East Lake MARTA station.
stories.body:
It only took about nine years, but Decatur's big bike infrastructure project is finally coming together. The West Howard Avenue Complete Streets project has been in the works since 2017, and now you can actually see what all the fuss was about. The roughly one-mile stretch from downtown Decatur to East Lake MARTA station looks completely different.

What they've built is pretty impressive: a two-way protected cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, better crosswalks, and traffic calming measures that should make West Howard feel less like a highway. This was always one of those streets where cars would fly through at 45+ mph even though it runs right past neighborhoods and schools.

The timing is perfect too, since so many people are trying to bike more and drive less. Having a protected connection between downtown Decatur and the MARTA station is huge for getting around without a car. Plus, this could be a model for other suburban cities that want to make their streets more people-friendly instead of just car sewers.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lanes on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Moves to Block ICE Detention Facilities
stories.summary: Atlanta City Council's Public Safety Committee unanimously approved resolutions to ban ICE detention facilities within city limits and require documentation when ICE agents are present during police operations. The full council votes April 20, while dozens of residents also criticized the mayor for not signing the firefighter union contract.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council is taking a stand against immigration detention facilities, and the timing couldn't be more pointed. The Public Safety Committee unanimously passed two resolutions: one blocking new ICE detention facilities in the city and another requiring APD to document when ICE agents are present during their operations. The full council votes April 20.

The meeting also turned into a forum for criticizing Mayor Dickens over the firefighter union contract. The council ratified the city's first-ever firefighter union agreement back in April 2025, but Dickens has refused to sign it. Dozens of residents and firefighters showed up to ask why he's dragging his feet on something the council already approved.

What's interesting is how these two issues intersect with what's happening at the airport right now. You've got ICE agents deployed to Hartsfield-Jackson while the council is trying to limit ICE's presence in the city. It's a perfect example of how federal and local priorities can clash, especially during a crisis like the shutdown.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with residents speaking about ICE detention facilities

stories.headline: SW Atlanta Residents Block Alcohol Sales at Controversial Gas Station
stories.summary: Southwest Atlanta residents won a partial victory when the developer of a controversial gas station on Greenbriar Parkway withdrew their alcohol license application. The community has been fighting the project, arguing it violates a city law banning new gas stations in their neighborhood.
stories.body:
Score one for southwest Atlanta residents who've been fighting that controversial gas station on Greenbriar Parkway. The developer pulled their application for an alcohol license just hours before a key zoning meeting, which feels like a small victory in what's been a long battle.

Faye Floyd, who chairs Neighborhood Planning Unit P, has been organizing opposition to this project for months. The community argues that the city's own law prohibits new gas stations in their area, but somehow this one got approved anyway. While they couldn't stop the gas station itself, blocking alcohol sales removes one major concern about the impact on the neighborhood.

What makes this case interesting is how it highlights the tension between development pressure and existing community protections. Southwest Atlanta has been dealing with a lot of unwanted commercial development lately, and residents are getting more organized about pushing back. This could set a precedent for other neighborhoods trying to enforce zoning rules that protect their character.
stories.imageAlt: Community meeting with residents opposing gas station development

stories.headline: 15-Year-Old Arrested After Gun Found at Cobb High School
stories.summary: Hillgrove High School went into lockdown Tuesday morning after a 15-year-old student brought a gun to campus. The weapon was detected by security systems, and the student was arrested within 12 minutes. No one was injured, but parents and students were shaken by the incident.
stories.body:
Another day, another school gun scare, this time at Hillgrove High School in Cobb County. A 15-year-old student brought a firearm to campus Tuesday morning, triggering a Code Red lockdown and sending parents into panic mode. The good news is that the school's weapon detection system worked exactly as intended, and police nabbed the kid within 12 minutes.

What's notable here is how quickly everything got resolved. From detection to arrest took just 12 minutes, which shows that when schools have the right systems and protocols in place, they can respond effectively. Still, those 12 minutes probably felt like hours for everyone inside the building.

This incident comes as Georgia lawmakers are pushing to expand weapon detection systems to all public schools statewide. Hillgrove's quick response might be Exhibit A for why that's necessary. But it also raises questions about why a 15-year-old had access to a gun in the first place. The arrest part is easy; the prevention part is what we still haven't figured out.
stories.imageAlt: Police vehicles outside Hillgrove High School during lockdown
